name: The Elder Scrolls® Online
description: Every legend starts somewhere and in The Elder Scrolls Online, it starts with you. Join millions of players in an online, fantasy RPG set in a vast living Elder Scrolls world.
requirements:
Minimum:
  • Requires a 64-bit processor and operating system
  • OS: Windows 10 64-bit
  • Processor: Intel® Core™ i5 2300 or AMD FX4350
  • Memory: 4 GB RAM
  • Graphics: Direct X 11.0 compliant video card with 1GB RAM (NVIDIA® GeForce® 560 or AMD Radeon™ 6870)
  • DirectX: Version 11
  • Network: Broadband Internet connection
  • Storage: 150 GB available space
  • Sound Card: DirectX compatible sound card
comment1:
The game is fun, with a great story, setting, writing, and voice acting. The graphics are outdated and it shows, but what matters to me is what’s under the skin. The combat is fun, it’s like WoW, and if you love WoW and the holy trinity, you’ll be hooked.

A glaring issue for me is the monetization. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, let’s unpack this. You have the base game, which is buy-to-play. You have DLCs, which are also buy-to-play. Simple math: I buy the game and the DLCs, right? Well, yes and no. You have two options, either subscribe to ESO+ to get all old DLCs (except the latest) along with godly benefits, or buy the deluxe edition that includes all old DLCs (except the latest) without the benefits. You are essentially forced into ESO+.

Moving on. You get a mount, hooray. Then you have to spend more than 180 real-life days maxing your riding skills to make it fast and capable or, you guessed it, buy it from the cash shop for $60 per horse skill. Why? This is a recurring theme throughout the game.

Luckily, I bought it for the story and side quests, and I got it on discount. I wouldn’t recommend this game even at 80% off because it’s a honey trap. Run. I have money, I just hate their practices.
comment2: I have played ESO since 4/2021 and system says I logged over 19,400 hours. I previously played Oblivion; Skyrim, and Morrowind, in that order and was indestructible, all powerful in all three in less than 2 years (total). ESO still provides challenges after 5 years. I am 76 and retired; yes I play a lot of hours a day, rarely miss a day. Some complain the game is become pay to win, I haven't found that to be true, yes they sell goodies that can improve your housing whims, and things that you could just wait or play for, but they do have employees to pay to keep the game going and it's not cheap to pay people or provide servers that hold your characters info (forever). I have and can afford to buy whatever i want, but I have probably (outside of the annual fee for Plus) used less than $200 dollars (some of that was from gifts from my family) for Miscellaneous housing items and in-game homes. Ive had Crashing problems in last month and the Support team has been helpful to resolve, but when Win11 stopped working, it wasn't ESO's or the game's fault. Remember it is a game (a very life like, addictive GAME). You do have to put time in to be Extremely good, but you are not likely to become a "Superman" in this game, maybe an Emperor, Master crafter, Housing guru, formidable opponent, sales entrepreneur, or just have fun. I play PVE and PVP and all events, and I die a lot (unlike real-life, where you only get one chance). If you are having problems with play, then search the internet, lost of podcasters with great ideas, even ask in game for help.  Love the game (statement and a command). Highly recommended.
comment3: Awesome game with so many different things to do. Caters to anyone's interests - fashion, house decorating, PvE, PvP, siege-related combat, questing, lore, harvesting, collecting, achievement hunting, etc. Can play solo or with others. So many amazing improvements in the last couple of years. I've been playing for around a decade now and I can't recommend it enough. Overall has a very kind and caring player base.
